Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'm 27, working part-time, so I had to squeeze studying into whatever gaps I could find. I learn best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often print materials and work through problems physically rather than sitting at a computer. Some weeks I'd cram in 19 hours, other weeks barely anything until deadlines loomed.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I couldn't stick to a regular schedule because my work shifts changed constantly. When assignments were due, I'd suddenly become hyper-focused and put in long hours. But during quieter periods, I'd drift and barely touch the course materials for days.
Q3: Usually it was either an assignment deadline approaching or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes a peer from the support group would message me asking about something, and that would remind me to log in. I wasn't one of those people who checked the LMS daily out of habit—it was more reactive than proactive.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine. The peer support program helped a bit because I'd log in to check discussion posts or respond to classmates. But it always felt like something I had to consciously decide to do rather than something automatic.
Q5: The discussion forums were surprisingly useful because talking things through with other students helped concepts click for me. I also liked any interactive elements or practice problems where I could actually do something rather than just read or watch. Videos were okay but I'd often zone out unless I was actively taking notes alongside them.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the longer reading materials and some of the supplementary videos. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what would directly help my assignments and exams. Some resources felt like nice-to-haves rather than essentials, so they got pushed aside constantly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could practice something hands-on or if other students said it was helpful, I'd engage with it. Abstract or theoretical stuff without clear application felt like a waste when I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required stuff got done because it had to—that's why my assignment completion was high. But optional materials? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, and later never came. There was always something more pressing demanding my attention.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, print them out, and work through them with a pen in hand. I'd also use YouTube videos and external tutorials that explained things differently. The LMS was more like a starting point—I'd grab what I needed and then study elsewhere.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through printed problem sets at my kitchen table for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. Or I'd be discussing concepts with a study buddy from the peer support group in person. All that learning was happening, but digitally I looked completely inactive.
Q11: Yeah, constantly. I'd watch a video once to get the gist, download the slides or transcript, then study from those physical copies repeatedly. My actual engagement with the material was way deeper than my one-time access would suggest. I'm just not someone who learns well staring at screens.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when work got demanding and I felt overwhelmed. My stress was pretty high throughout, and my resilience isn't great—when things piled up, I'd almost shut down. The peer support helped pull me back, honestly. Without those check-ins, I might have drifted away completely.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with money stress, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. Competing responsibilities meant I'd do the bare minimum online—grab what I needed and log off. My engagement level probably looked terrible even when I was actually keeping up with the material.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything, or have a video playing while my mind was elsewhere. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't actually engaged. It was more about going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt less urgent than assignments. Even though I knew participating mattered, I'd convince myself I'd do it later. The anxiety of putting my thoughts out there combined with time pressure meant I'd avoid it until I absolutely couldn't anymore.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd have this burst of LMS activity right before things were due—downloading materials, checking rubrics, submitting work. Without deadlines, I'd drift. It's not great, but that external pressure was what kept me on track. My whole engagement pattern was deadline-driven.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to engage more with the next assignment. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd feel disconnected from the course. The peer support interactions were actually more immediate and helpful than formal feedback sometimes. Knowing someone was paying attention made a difference.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work around my schedule, bad because without rigid structure, I'd procrastinate. Having 15 online courses meant I was constantly juggling, and this course sometimes got deprioritized despite my interest in it.
Q19: The kinesthetic, hands-on activities were easier to engage with—anything where I could practice and apply concepts. What made it harder was heavy reading requirements and passive video lectures. Also, the LMS interface wasn't the most intuitive, which added friction when I was already low on motivation.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need, exploring different resources, or just struggling to understand where to start. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. They might be clicking around trying to figure things out, or maybe they're dealing with anxiety that makes them check things repeatedly without actually completing work.
Q21: That's basically me. My LMS activity was low, but I performed well because I was studying extensively offline. I'd grab materials, work through them physically, discuss with peers outside the platform, and show up prepared for assessments. The LMS just doesn't capture that kind of learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, whether you submit things on time. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, peer conversations, and the mental state behind the clicks. Someone could look disengaged but be deeply learning, or look active but be completely lost.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, asking questions, connecting concepts to real life. For me, engagement often happened away from the screen—when I was working through problems hands-on or discussing ideas with classmates. It's internal, not just behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how the peer support program kept me from completely disengaging when things got hard. It wouldn't capture my offline study sessions, my conversations with classmates, or the fact that despite low motivation scores, I genuinely wanted to do well. It also wouldn't show the stress and anxiety I managed while still achieving a distinction.
